content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 8, 1991


Richard A. McManus
Chief, Cal-Mortgage Loan
Guarantee Program
1600 9th Street, Room 400
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. I-91-223

Dear Mr. McManus:

You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").1/  Your request is
one for general assistance.  Accordingly, we are treating it as a
request for informal assistance.2/

QUESTION

You have asked the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") to advise you if the procedures set out in 2
California Code of Regulations Section 91199 comport with all
state statutory requirements for the reporting of financial
interests.  The purpose of these procedures is to permit the
public to make its own informed judgement regarding conflicts, and
to prohibit advisory loan committee members from participating in
decision making when an actual conflict exists.

CONCLUSION

The members of the Advisory Loan Committee (the "Committee")
are currently designated in the conflict of interest code for the
Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (the

_________________________
1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-223
Page 2

"Office"). It is through the mechanism of this conflict of
interest code that the requirements set out in Government Code
Section 87300, as well as Sections 87500 and 87100, are met and
not through Regulation 91499.

FACTS

The Office is currently responsible for the administration of
a program to provide loan insurance for construction loans for
eligible health facilities. The authority for this program is
found in Chapter 4, Part 1, Division 1 of the Health and Safety
Code. It is within the Office that the Advisory Loan Committee
was created (2 Cal. Code of Regs. 91499.) A decision by the
Committee to insure a project affects the relative credit
worthiness of a health facility construction project.

The Committee is appointed by the Office and is comprised of
two governmental members appointed by the Office as well as five
members appointed from outside state government. The latter
members are by profession: one hospital administrator; two
investment bankers; and two certified public accountants. Each
advisory member's professional activities require daily
interaction with the health industry. In 1989, the Committee was
restructured to include two alternate members who are required to
attend and participate at all meetings. Alternates do not,
however, have full voting rights until such time that they are
called upon to replace an absent term member.

The Committee is designated in the Office's conflict of
interest code and as such its members are required to file
statements of economic interests based upon the reporting
requirements assigned in the disclosure category portion of that
conflict of interest code. Alternates are not covered by the
Office's current conflict of interest code.

ANALYSIS

The Act at Section 87300 requires each governmental agency to
adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. A conflict of
interest code is a document which lists the decisionmaking
positions within an agency and further provides disclosure
categories which describe the type of information to be reported
by the employees serving in these decisionmaking positions. The
categories are designed to require disclosure of the financial
interests which the designated employee can affect by decisions he
or she makes in his or her official capacity. A conflict of
interest code has the force of law and any violation of the code
by a designated employee is considered to be a violation of the
Act. (Section 87300.) Every conflict of interest code must
require a designated employee's disqualification from
participation in any decision in which the employee has a
financial interest unless the decision could not legally be acted
upon or decided without his or her participation. (Section
87302(c).)

Our File No. I-91-223
Page 3

are currently designated in the conflict of interest code for the
Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (the
"Office"). It is through the mechanism of this conflict of
interest code that the requirements set out in Government Code
Section 87300, as well as Sections 87500 and 87100, are met and
not through Regulation 91499.3/

Based upon the documentation submitted with your April 4,
1991 letter, it appears that the Committee has been restructured
to include two co-chairs. As the co-chairs are participating in
the decisionmaking process for the Committee, regardless of their
eligibility to vote, and are making decisions by virtue of their
participation in the discussion and critique of agenda items, they
should also be designated in the Office's conflict of interest
code.

Any employee who exercises decisionmaking authority must be
included in the list of designated employees if he or she makes
decisions whether or not to obligate or commit his or her agency
to a course of action (Regulation 13700(b)(3)), or, if, he or
she:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with
a governmental entity or private person regarding the
decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker,
either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation
which requires the exercise of judgment on the part
of the official or designated employee and the
purpose of which is to influence the decision, or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which is to
influence the decision.

Regulation 13700(c)(1) and (c)(2).
(Emphasis added.)

3/ Regulation 91499 simply states the structure of the Committee
and the requirement that its members complete statements of
economic interests and disqualify themselves from issues which may
present a conflict of interest. It does not contain the authority
to require disclosure for disqualification.

Our File No. I-91-223
Page 4


As the Office will soon be amending its conflict of interest
code, we ask that you notify the Office that its code should be
amended to include these positions.

Additionally, I have enclosed a pamphlet titled "A Guide to
the Political Reform Act of 1974: California's Conflict of
Interest Law for Public Officials". This pamphlet is intended to
help public officials understand and abide by the laws concerning
disclosure and disqualification. It is somewhat general in
nature, yet can be used as a guide for members of the public who
are concerned about maintaining impartial government
decisionmaking.

If you would like to obtain additional copies of this
pamphlet, or would like to discuss the contents of this letter,
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Cheryl A. Hoff
Staff Services Analyst
Legal Division

SH:CAH:ch

Enclosures

sections.analysis:
The Act at Section 87300 requires each governmental agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. A conflict of interest code is a document which lists the decisionmaking positions within an agency and further provides disclosure categories which describe the type of information to be reported by the employees serving in these decisionmaking positions. The categories are designed to require disclosure of the financial interests which the designated employee can affect by decisions he or she makes in his or her official capacity. A conflict of interest code has the force of law and any violation of the code by a designated employee is considered to be a violation of the Act. (Section 87300.) Every conflict of interest code must require a designated employee's disqualification from participation in any decision in which the employee has a financial interest unless the decision could not legally be acted upon or decided without his or her participation. (Section 87302(c).)

The members of the Advisory Loan Committee (the "Committee") are currently designated in the conflict of interest code for the Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (the "Office"). It is through the mechanism of this conflict of interest code that the requirements set out in Government Code Section 87300, as well as Sections 87500 and 87100, are met and not through Regulation 91499.3/

Based upon the documentation submitted with your April 4, 1991 letter, it appears that the Committee has been restructured to include two alternates. As the alternates are participating in the decisionmaking process for the Committee, regardless of their eligibility to vote, and are making decisions by virtue of their participation in the discussion and critique of agenda items, they should also be designated in the Office's conflict of interest code.

Any employee who exercises decisionmaking authority must be included in the list of designated employees if he or she makes decisions whether or not to obligate or commit his or her agency to a course of action (Regulation 18700(b)(3)), or, if, he or she:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision, or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c)(1) and (c)(2).
(Emphasis added.)

3/ Regulation 91499 simply states the structure of the Committee and the requirement that its members complete statements of economic interests and disqualify themselves from issues which may present a conflict of interest. It does not contain the authority to require disclosure for disqualification.

As the Office will soon be amending its conflict of interest code, we ask that you notify the Office that its code should be amended to include these positions.

Additionally, I have enclosed a pamphlet titled "A Guide to the Political Reform Act of 1974: California's Conflict of Interest Law for Public Officials". This pamphlet is intended to help public officials understand and abide by the laws concerning disclosure and disqualification. It is somewhat general in nature, yet can be used as a guide for members of the public who are concerned about maintaining impartial government decisionmaking.

If you would like to obtain additional copies of this pamphlet, or would like to discuss the contents of this letter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.
